title: DNIT BBNL
content:
Detailed Notice Inviting Tender (DNIT)

1. Introduction & Scope of Work

  • Issuer: BSNL (Bharat Sanchar Nigam Limited) on behalf of the Universal Service Obligation Fund (USOF), Department of Telecommunications (DoT).

  • Project Goal: Design, Supply, Construction, Installation, Upgradation, Operation and Maintenance (O&M) of the middle mile network of BharatNet under the Design Build Operate and Maintain (DBOM) Model.

  • Geographical Breakdown: The project is divided into 16 Packages covering various States/UTs.

2. Package Categories & Bid Security

The 16 packages are classified into two major categories.:

  • Category 1A (Packages 1 to 4): MP/DNH/DD, UP(East), Rajasthan, Karnataka/Goa/Puducherry. (Bid Security: Rs. 20 Crore per package)

  • Category 1B (Packages 5 to 8): Uttarakhand, UP(West), NER-II (Arunachal, Nagaland, Manipur), Bihar. (Bid Security: Rs. 20 Crore per package)

  • Category 2 (Packages 9 to 16): HP, WB/Sikkim/A&N, J&K/Ladakh, Punjab, Haryana, NER-I (Mizoram, Tripura), Assam, Kerala. (Bid Security: Rs. 10 Crore per package)

3. Key Definitions in the Contract

Section II outlines important acronyms and terms that will be used throughout the project:

  • PIA (Project Implementation Agency) / Contractor: The successful bidder (single entity or consortium) awarded the contract.

  • FMA (Fiber Maintenance Agency): The existing agency handling the AMC of the current network.

  • PMA (Planned Maintenance Agency): The existing agency handling overhead OFC replacement works.

  • Independent Engineer (IE): A Third-Party Agency appointed by BSNL for acceptance testing, inspection, bill verification, and dispute resolution.

  • APO (Advance Purchase Order): The initial letter of intent indicating BSNL’s intention to award the work.

  • Firm Work Order: The actual executable order issued by the BSNL Circle Chief General Manager (CGM) after the PIA completes the survey and BSNL approves the Bill of Quantity (BOQ). Payments are based entirely on this Firm Work Order.

4. Banning of Business & Penal Actions – Appendix-1

BSNL provides a detailed matrix of actions for specific defaults. Submitting fake/forged documents or bank instruments carries severe penalties depending on when the fraud is detected:

  • Detection Prior to Award of APO: Rejection of bid, forfeiture of EMD, and banning of business for up to 3 years.

  • Detection After APO but before PG/SD receipt: Cancellation of APO, rejection of bid, forfeiture of EMD, and a 3-year ban.

  • Detection After receipt of PG/SD: Cancellation of APO, forfeiture of the Performance Guarantee (PG)/Security Deposit (SD), and a 3-year ban.

  • Detection After issue of Purchase Order (PO): Termination/Short Closure of the PO, forfeiture of PG/SD, and a 3-year ban. (Note: BSNL will settle bills for materials already received in correct quantity/quality if they are usable).

  • Coercive Behaviour: If a vendor uses violent/coercive means (physical or verbal threats) or obstructs BSNL executives or other bidders during the tender process, they will face a 3-year ban on business dealings.

5. Failures in Delivery, Commissioning & Execution

  • Failure to Accept APO: If the L1 bidder fails to accept the APO/AWO and submit the PBG within the specified time, their EMD is forfeited. Crucial rule for MSEs: If an MSE bidder claimed an EMD exemption and defaults here, they will be banned from future BSNL tenders for 1 year.

  • Failure to Supply/Execute (Total or Partial): If the vendor fails to commission the equipment or execute the work even within extended delivery schedules:

    • BSNL will terminate or short-close the PO/WO.

    • BSNL will execute the balance work/purchases at the Risk & Cost of the defaulting vendor.

    • Excess charges incurred by BSNL will be recovered from the vendor's PG/SD or any outstanding bills.

6. Quality Problems & Fraudulent Claims

  • Substandard Equipment: If supplied equipment fails to perform satisfactorily in the field:

    • Not Acceptable: Material is returned, and cost is recovered from outstanding bills or PG/SD.

    • Usable but Degraded: If the material is already inducted and cannot be returned, BSNL will accept it with a Financial Penalty (Price of equipment minus a determined degraded price).

  • Major Quality Defects: Persistent quality issues will lead to the withdrawal of the Type Approval (TSEC/IA) by the QA circle, alongside financial recoveries.

  • Over-billing/Fraudulent Claims: If a vendor submits claims for amounts already paid or for quantities in excess of what was actually supplied, BSNL will recover the over-payment from outstanding dues, EMD, or PG by invoking the ‘Set off’ clause.

7. Network Security, Sabotage, & Privacy Breaches

BSNL imposes strict penalties for any actions that threaten the integrity of the network or the nation.

  • Triggering Offenses:

    • Tampering with hardware/software/firmware that adversely affects normal BSNL/TSP equipment working.

    • Disrupting or sabotaging BSNL network equipment (Exchanges, BTS, BSC/MSC, transmission equipment, etc.).

    • Tampering with billing, invoicing, or customer accounts.

    • Hacking BSNL customer accounts to threaten others or spread improper news.

    • Any action that endangers the security of India.

  • Penal Actions: Immediate termination of the Purchase Order (PO) / Work Order (WO), a 3-year ban on business dealings, recovery of any financial loss from the vendor’s Performance Guarantee (PG)/Security Deposit (SD) or bills, and legal action if required.

8. Bankruptcy, Insolvency & Criminal Convictions

  • Bankruptcy/Insolvency: If the vendor is declared bankrupt, insolvent, or if a limited company is wound up/liquidated, BSNL will terminate/short-close the PO.

    • BSNL will only settle bills for materials already received that are of correct quality and quantity. No further supplies will be accepted.

    • For turnkey projects, if the material is commissioned and usable, BSNL will pay for it. If it operates with degraded performance, BSNL will apply a financial penalty to determine a "degraded price."

  • Criminal Conviction: If the vendor, proprietor, director, or partner is convicted by a Court of Law for offenses involving moral turpitude in business dealings, the contract will be immediately terminated. Settlement of bills will follow the same rules as in bankruptcy cases.

9. Refusal to Pay Dues & General Malpractices

  • Defying Authorities: If a vendor refuses to return BSNL’s dues despite an Arbitrator's order or a Court Order, the contract will be terminated. The vendor will face a 3-year ban or until the dues are cleared (whichever is later), and BSNL will take legal recourse.

  • Agency Directives: If agencies like the CBI, Independent External Monitor (IEM), Income Tax, GST, or Customs Departments recommend action against a vendor, BSNL will execute actions as per their directions.

  • Other Grounds for a 3-Year Ban:

    • Strong justification that the vendor is guilty of bribery, corruption, fraud, interpolation, or misrepresentation.

    • Failure to execute the contract satisfactorily or submit required documents.

  • Ongoing Works Exception: A banning order does not affect existing, ongoing works or AMC/CAMC contracts already in place; those will continue along with the settlement of their respective bills.

10. Performance Security / Bank Guarantee

This is a critical financial safeguard for BSNL to ensure the winning bidder fulfills their obligations.

  • Standard Requirement: All successful bidders (including MSEs) must furnish a Performance Security equal to 5% of the Advance Work Order value within 14 days of the APO issue date.

  • Abnormally Low Bids: If the bidder's quoted price is 15% or more below BSNL’s internal estimated cost, the Performance Security requirement is doubled to 10% of the APO value.

  • Consortium Rule: In a consortium, the Lead Member must furnish at least 50% of the total required Performance Security.

  • Validity: The Bank Guarantee or Insurance Surety Bond must be valid for 3.5 years and must be extended as required (up to 6 months beyond any project extensions). It will be discharged only after the successful completion of all obligations, including the warranty period.

11. Inspections and Tests

  • Purchaser's Right: BSNL (or its representative/Independent Engineer) retains the right to inspect and test the goods/works to ensure they conform to specifications.

  • Vendor's Obligation: The supplier must provide all reasonable facilities and assistance, including testing instruments, access to drawings, and production data, to the inspectors at no extra charge.

12. Inspection, Delivery, and Commissioning

  • Pre- and Post-Installation Testing: BSNL has the right to inspect goods before and after installation. If any equipment fails testing before "take over," the inspector will issue a notice.

  • Defect Rectification: The PIA must fix or replace defective equipment within a maximum of 3 months of the initial report, completely free of charge. If they fail, BSNL can replace it at the PIA's cost.

  • Commissioning Certificate: Issued within 6 weeks of successful performance tests.

    • Note: Minor defects that do not affect commercial use will not delay the certificate, but the PIA must fix these minor defects within 6 months.

  • Delivery Extensions: BSNL can grant delivery extensions, but reserves the absolute right to revise prices and levy delay penalties.

17. Training, Incidental Services, & Spares

  • Training Obligations: The PIA must train BSNL/USOF supervisory staff on Quality, Acceptance Testing (AT), and active network elements (Routers, SNOC, RFMS) free of cost.

    • Volume: 1 man-week of training for every 200 GPs per year (for the first 2 years) and 1 man-week per 400 GPs thereafter.

    • Material: PIA must provide video training material (around 100 hours for Category-1 packages and 50 hours for Category-2 packages).

    • Logistics: PIA arranges the venue and materials; trainees cover their own travel and stay.

  • 10-Year Spares Agreement (Crucial Clause): The PIA must enter an agreement with OEMs to ensure spare parts support for the entire 10-year contract lifetime.

    • Obsolescence Rule: If an equipment cannot be repaired because spare parts are no longer available, the PIA must replace the product with an equal or upgraded/higher version entirely free of cost.

18. Warranty Terms

  • Warranty Start Date (Routers & Hardware): The warranty does not start immediately upon physical installation. It starts on the 1st day of the next calendar quarter (Jan 1, Apr 1, Jul 1, or Oct 1) after 90% of the GPs in a block (including the block router) are commissioned and visible in the NOC.

  • Warranty Start Date (Other Equipment): For items like Microwave, UBR, and State NOC electronics, warranty starts on the 1st day of the next calendar quarter following successful Acceptance Testing.

  • Scope of Warranty: Goods must be new, defect-free, and of the highest grade. If a defective part is replaced during the warranty, the warranty resets for that specific part. Replacements must be done at the site free of all charges, including freight and insurance.

19. Documents Required for Claiming Payment

To claim payments, the PIA must submit specific documentation to the paying authority:

  1. Invoices: Must be in the name of USOF, DoT (or designated authority) with a clear breakout of Basic Price, GST, and other duties.

  2. QA Inspection Certificate (as applicable).

  3. Acceptance Testing (AT) Certificate (Proof of completion).

  4. E-waybill & Proof of GST payment.

  5. Electronic Payment Mandate: Required for payments of Rs. 5 Lakhs and above (includes Bank Name, Branch, IFSC, MICR, and Account Number).

20. Strict GST Compliance & Indemnification

BSNL mandates rigorous adherence to GST laws to protect its Input Tax Credit (ITC):

  • Payment Release tied to Filing: The tax portion of the payment will only be released to the PIA after they have successfully declared the invoice details in their GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B returns and submitted proof of compliance.

  • TDS/TCS: BSNL will deduct applicable Income Tax and GST TDS/TCS on behalf of USOF.

  • Penalty for Mismatches: The PIA is solely responsible for ensuring that outward supply returns are filed correctly. If there is a mismatch on the GSTN portal due to the supplier's fault resulting in a loss of Input Tax Credit for USOF/DoT, the PIA must indemnify the purchaser for the lost credit plus interest.

  • Recovery of Tax Losses: BSNL explicitly reserves the right to adjust or forfeit the PIA's Bank Guarantee to recover any financial losses caused by the PIA's non-compliance with tax laws.

21. Final GST Rules & Contract Pricing

  • GST Blacklisting: Bidders must provide a self-declaration that they are not blacklisted by GST authorities. If a supplier gets blacklisted during the contract tenure, they must indemnify USOF/DoT against any losses.

  • State-wise Invoicing: If the project spans multiple states, the PIA must submit separate, state-wise invoices.

  • Firm Pricing & Tax Variations: Prices are fixed at the time the Purchase/Work Order is issued.

    • If statutory taxes/duties decrease during the scheduled delivery period, the benefit must be passed on to BSNL.

    • If statutory taxes/duties increase during the scheduled delivery period, BSNL will pay the revised, higher price.

    • Crucial Exception: If taxes increase after the expiry of the scheduled delivery date (i.e., the PIA is delayed), the supplier bears the cost of the increase. However, if taxes decrease during a delayed period, BSNL still gets the benefit.

22. Changes to Orders & Sub-Contracting Limits

  • Order Modifications: BSNL (on behalf of USOF) can alter drawings, designs, specifications, or services. If this changes the cost or time required, an equitable adjustment will be made, provided the PIA requests it within 30 days of receiving the change order.

  • Sub-Contracting Rules:

    • The PIA can sub-contract up to a maximum of 70% of the contract value for services (excluding material costs). Prior approval from BSNL is mandatory.

    • Maximum Sub-contractors allowed: 15 for Category 1A & 1B packages; 12 for Category 2 packages.

    • Eligibility of Sub-contractors: They must meet at least 50% of the proportionate technical eligibility criteria mentioned in the tender.

    • Despite sub-contracting, the PIA remains 100% liable and responsible for the quality of goods, services, and SLA compliance.

23. Delays, Extensions, and Penalties

  • Standard Penalties: Delaying the project renders the PIA liable to forfeiture of the Performance Bank Guarantee (PBG), imposition of liquidated damages/penalties, and short-closure or termination of the contract.

  • Risk & Cost Execution: If BSNL cancels or short-closes the order due to PIA default, BSNL can get the remaining work done by another agency at the Risk and Cost of the defaulting PIA (valid for one year from the date of short-closure or scheduled completion).

  • Time Extensions:

    • If the PIA foresees a delay, they must promptly notify BSNL.

    • BSNL can grant an extension of up to 4 weeks without severe conditions, provided the PIA submits a request and accepts standard penalties.

    • Extensions beyond 4 weeks: The PIA must submit an Additional Bank Guarantee equal to 3% of the balance quantity's value. This extra BG must be valid for 6 months beyond the newly requested extension period.

    • If the PIA fails to complete the work even in the extended period, BSNL will short-close the contract and forfeit both the main PBG and the additional 3% BG.

24. Force Majeure & Termination Clauses

  • Force Majeure: Covers uncontrollable events like war, public enemy acts, civil commotion, sabotage, fires, floods, explosions, epidemics, quarantine, or Acts of God.

    • The affected party must notify the other within 10 days of the event occurring.

    • If the event lasts for more than 60 days, either party can terminate the contract without financial penalty, and BSNL will pay for undamaged, acceptable materials already manufactured/supplied.

  • Termination for Default: BSNL can terminate the contract if the PIA:

    • Fails to deliver goods/services within the specified time.

    • Fails to remedy a breach within 15 days of receiving a default notice.

    • SLA Failure: If the average monthly SLA (Gram Panchayat network uptime) falls below 90% for 3 consecutive months on the newly constructed or handed-over network.

  • Termination for Insolvency: If the PIA goes bankrupt or becomes insolvent, BSNL can terminate the contract immediately without compensation to the PIA.

  • Termination for Convenience: BSNL/USOF can terminate the contract at any time for their convenience by giving 3 months' prior written notice.

25. Dispute Resolution Mechanism

If a dispute arises, both parties are encouraged to resolve it amicably. If direct negotiation fails, a tiered resolution process is triggered:

  • Tier 1 - Dispute Resolution Committee (DRC): A package-wise committee is formed, consisting of:

    1. BSNL - BharatNet BA (Business Area) Head

    2. PIA - Authorized Representative

    3. Independent Engineer (IE) - Authorized Representative

  • Tier 2 - Dispute Adjudication Board (DAB): If the DRC fails to resolve the issue within 15 days of the dispute notice, it is escalated to the DAB. (The composition of the DAB continues on the next page).

26. Escalation of Dispute Resolution

If the Tier-1 Dispute Resolution Committee (DRC) fails to resolve the issue within 15 days, the dispute escalates to higher authorities:

  • Tier 2 - Dispute Adjudication Board (DAB):

    • Established on a package-wise basis.

    • Composition: BSNL Chief General Manager (CGM), PIA Authorized State Representative, and IE Authorized State Representative.

  • Tier 3 - Outside Expert Committee (OEC) for Conciliation:

    • If the DAB also fails, the aggrieved party (Claimant) refers the dispute to the OEC.

    • Composition: 3 independent members appointed by USOF/BSNL.

    • Condition Precedent: Parties must ensure no parallel court/tribunal proceedings are ongoing for the same dispute.

27. Rules and Timelines for OEC Conciliation

The OEC process is designed to be fast, strictly documented, and confidential:

  • Strict Timelines:

    • Claimant submits the statement of claims within 30 days.

    • Respondent (BSNL/USOF) replies/counter-claims within the next 30 days.

    • Rejoinders (if any) within the next 15 days.

  • Representation: No advocates or outside legal consultants are allowed. Parties must be represented only by their in-house employees/executives. Ex-officers of BSNL/USOF who handled the matter previously cannot represent the contractor.

  • Evidence & Meetings: The OEC relies only on documentary evidence (no oral evidence). The proceedings should generally conclude within 4 meetings or 60 days.

  • Outcome: OEC recommendations are non-binding.

    • Parties have 15 days to accept or reject them.

    • If accepted by both parties, a Settlement Agreement is signed under Section 73 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, giving it the binding force of an arbitral award.

  • Confidentiality: All OEC proceedings, admissions, and proposals are strictly confidential and cannot be used as evidence in future court/arbitration proceedings.

28. SLA-Based Termination & Handover Rules

  • Post-Expiry Obligations: If BSNL decides not to extend the contract, they will notify the PIA at least 3 months prior to expiry. The PIA must continue running the network operations until BSNL appoints a new contractor or builds its own infrastructure. (The PIA will be paid for this extended execution period).

  • Termination for Poor SLA: BSNL has the right to terminate the contract entirely if the network SLA achievement falls:

    • Below 90% for 3 consecutive months.

    • Below 80% for 2 consecutive months.

29. Third-Party Damages, Utilities & RoW

The PIA must handle public infrastructure with extreme care.

  • Third-Party Damage: If the PIA damages other service providers' cables, sewage lines, electricity lines, or roads, the PIA is 100% liable for the damage charges and penalties. BSNL will deduct these costs from the PIA's bills.

  • Shifting of Obstructing Utilities:

    • The PIA must first explore an alternate path (e.g., using ADSS OFC).

    • If shifting water pipes, electric lines, etc., is unavoidable, the PIA undertakes the shifting with BSNL's assistance.

    • The cost of shifting utilities is borne by USOF/DoT/BSNL.

  • Felling of Trees: If trees cause a material adverse effect on the project, BSNL will assist in getting permits. Costs and revenues from tree felling belong to USOF/BSNL.

  • Public Inconvenience: The PIA cannot dump empty cable drums or waste in public places. Waste must be disposed of within 3 days. Failure to do so allows BSNL to dispose of it and charge the PIA a penalty of up to Rs. 1,000 per default, plus disposal costs.

30. Strict Penalties for Damaging Existing Cables

During trenching, if the PIA cuts or damages existing BSNL underground cables, severe penalties apply:

  • Copper UG Cable: Rs. 50,000 per damage instance.

  • OFC (Any size): Rs. 1,00,000 per damage instance.

  • Miscellaneous (Electrical, Water, Sewage, etc.): Rs. 1,00,000 per instance.

  • Repair Rule: The PIA must repair the damaged cable free of charge (bearing labor + jointing kit costs).

  • Penalty Waiver/Reduction: If the PIA repairs the damage within 72 hours and submits a "No Objection Certificate" (NOC) within 10 days, the penalty is reduced to 20% of the standard penalty amount.

31. Comprehensive Indemnities & IPR Protection

The PIA must sign a blanket indemnity protecting BSNL, USOF, and DoT ("Authority Indemnified Persons") from any financial or legal liabilities.

  • General Indemnities: The PIA covers all costs, damages, or suits arising from:

    • Failure to comply with laws or permits.

    • Unpaid taxes or unpaid subcontractors.

    • Fraud, gross negligence, or willful misconduct by the PIA's staff.

    • Personal injury, death, or property damage during the project.

  • Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) Indemnity:

    • The PIA indemnifies BSNL against any claims of patent, copyright, or trademark infringement related to the supplied equipment or software.

    • If an injunction prevents BSNL from using the network due to an IP dispute, the PIA must (at its own cost) either secure a license for BSNL to continue using it, replace the infringing part, or modify it to be non-infringing.

  • Notice of Claims: If BSNL receives a claim, they will notify the PIA within 15 days. The PIA will then have the obligation to take over the legal defense at their own cost, using their own counsel, provided there is no conflict of interest.

32. Defence of Claims & Limitation of Liability

  • Legal Defense: If a third party files a claim (e.g., for injury, property damage, or IP infringement), the PIA (Indemnifying Party) has the right to take over the legal defense at their own expense. If the PIA takes over, BSNL cannot settle the claim without the PIA's consent.

  • No Consequential Claims: Neither party can claim indirect, incidental, or consequential damages (such as loss of profit) under the indemnity clauses.

  • Limitation of Liability (Crucial Cap): The overall financial liability of either party (BSNL or PIA) is strictly capped at the Total Cost of the Project awarded to the PIA.

  • Exceptions to the Cap: The PIA’s liability becomes uncapped (unlimited) in cases involving:

    • Fraud, gross negligence, or willful misconduct.

    • Breach of Applicable Laws or Permits.

    • Intellectual Property (IP) Rights violations.

    • Bodily injury or death.

    • Physical damage to BSNL’s or a third party’s property.

33. Easements, Permits, RoW, and Hardships

  • Procuring Permits: The PIA is entirely responsible for applying for and securing all necessary Right of Way (RoW), easements, and crossing permits (Highway, Railway, Forest, Canal, etc.) at their own cost.

  • Payment of Fees: BSNL will pay the actual statutory fees/demand notes raised by the authorities against the bills. However, no extra charges will be paid to the PIA for the administrative effort of pursuing these permits.

  • No Hardship Compensation: The PIA will not get extra money or compensation for encountering harsh conditions, such as having to route cables next to other pipelines, across cliffs/streams, or dealing with rocky embankments.

34. Public Safety & Protection of Existing Facilities

The PIA must execute construction without endangering the public or disrupting existing infrastructure.

  • Warning Signs & Traffic: When excavating near traffic, the PIA must place warning boards (minimum size 3' x 2'), traffic signals, red flags, and barricades.

  • Penalty for Negligence: If the PIA fails to put up proper warning signals or barricades, BSNL will levy a penalty of Rs. 1,000/- per day until the issue is fixed.

  • Underground Utilities: The PIA must use approved, non-destructive methods to locate existing water mains, gas pipes, and telecom cables before digging.

  • Total Financial Liability: If the PIA damages any underground or above-ground structures, they are solely responsible for 100% of the repair costs and must pay the current market value for any lost commodities (e.g., lost water or gas).

35. Labor Welfare & Statutory Compliances

BSNL explicitly absolves itself from any employer-employee relationship with the PIA's workforce.

  • Licenses & Child Labor: The PIA must obtain a valid labor license under the Contract Labour (R&A) Act 1970 before starting work. They must strictly abide by the Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act 1986.

  • Timely Wages & Dues: The PIA must pay wages to workers on a regular and timely basis (by the 7th of every month). They must fully comply with EPF, ESI, Workmen's Compensation, and Bonus Acts.

  • Zero Liability for BSNL: In case of any site accident or death, BSNL (as the Principal Employer) will not be responsible for paying any workmen's compensation. The PIA bears sole responsibility.

  • General Law Compliance: The PIA must comply with all local, municipal, state, and national laws and bear all payroll taxes for their employees.

36. Insurance & PIA's Office Setup

  • Comprehensive Insurance: The PIA is mandated to take out and maintain comprehensive insurance at their own expense. This must cover:

    • Third-party risk.

    • Plant, machinery, men, and materials at the site.

    • Workmen’s compensation.

  • Proof of Insurance: The PIA must furnish original or attested copies of the insurance policies and premium receipts within 15 days if BSNL asks for them.

  • Establishment of Office: The PIA must set up an operational office equipped with a landline/mobile phone within 7 days from the issuance of the Work Order.

37. PIA’s Local Office Setup

Following up on the requirement to establish an operational office within 7 days of the Work Order:

  • Infrastructure: The office must be equipped with an E-mail facility and a PC.

  • Location & Personnel: The Project Implementation Agency (PIA) must establish at least one office in each BSNL Business Area (BA) and appoint one nodal person in each district. Additional offices can be opened based on operational needs.

38. Project Background & Previous Phases

This marks the beginning of the Special (Commercial) Conditions of Contract (SCC).

  • BharatNet Objective: Funded by the Universal Service Obligation Fund (USOF) / DoT to provide high-speed broadband to all inhabited Gram Panchayats (GPs) and villages. It operates on a non-discriminatory basis for all service providers.

  • Phase I History: Implemented by CPSUs (BSNL, PGCIL, RailTel) using existing BSNL block-level fiber and laying incremental underground OFC to GPs. It used a linear topology with GPON electronics.

  • Phase II History: Implemented via CPSU, BBNL, and State-led models. It involved end-to-end OFC laying from Blocks to GPs using both linear (GPON) and ring (IP-MPLS) topologies, alongside aerial, underground, satellite, and radio media.

39. Detailed Scope of Work

The Project Implementation Agency (PIA) will execute this project under the Design, Build, Operate and Maintain (DBOM) model. The core scope includes:

  1. Construction: Building new network infrastructure to connect remaining GPs and Blocks.

  2. Upgradation (Crucial): Upgrading the existing linear GPON infrastructure to a robust, redundant Ring Topology using IP-MPLS routers.

  3. Operation & Maintenance (O&M): Taking over the existing network and maintaining both the old and newly deployed infrastructure according to strict Service Level Agreements (SLAs).

  4. State NOC: Establishing a State Network Operations Centre (S-NOC) to monitor, supervise, and provision services across the network.

  5. Service Provisioning: Enabling retail, enterprise, and wholesale services for BSNL.

40. Ring Architecture & Laying Guidelines

  • Primary Method: The PIA must prioritize Underground (UG) OFC laying.

  • Alternative Method (ADSS): If UG laying is not feasible, the PIA can lay Aerial ADSS OFC (preferably using existing electricity infrastructure). This requires explicit justification, recommendation from the Independent Engineer (IE), and final approval from BSNL.

  • Satellite Migration: GPs currently connected via Satellite media under Phase-II will be migrated to the OFC network.

41. Handover / Takeover of the Existing Network

A critical phase of the project is the PIA taking over the massive existing BSNL network.

  • "Good Condition" Standard: BSNL (via its existing maintenance agencies, FMA/PMA) will hand over the existing network block-wise. The network is considered in "good condition" if the optical power at the GP is between -8 dBm and -25 dBm.

  • Handover Timelines (Table X.1): The existing network must be progressively handed over to the PIA within a strict schedule from the Agreement signing date (T):

    • 25% GPs: T + 1 month

    • 50% GPs: T + 3 months

    • 75% GPs: T + 5 months

    • 90% GPs: T + 7 months

    • (Note: The remaining 10% timeline is continued on the next page).

42. Final Handover Timelines & Equipment Takeover

  • 100% Handover Deadline: Continuing from the previous table, the entire existing network (100% of GPs) must be handed over to the PIA within T + 9 months (where T is the Agreement signing date).

  • GP End Equipment: BSNL will hand over the GP equipment (ONT, Battery, Solar Panel, etc.) block-wise. The crucial baseline is that the optical power at the GP ONT must be -25 dBm or better. The PIA will get access to the NMS/GIS to verify status.

  • Block End Equipment (OLTs): BSNL will hand over the existing OLTs to the PIA. The PIA is strictly responsible for the First Line Maintenance (FLM) of these OLTs until the network traffic is successfully migrated to the newly installed IP-MPLS routers.

  • OFC Network & Faults:

    • The OFC routes are handed over "as is where is" based on signed L-14 diagrams.

    • If a route is faulty, the outgoing maintenance agency (FMA/PMA) has 3 months from the first GP handover date to fix it.

    • If the outgoing agency fails to fix it within 3 months, the PIA is bound to take over the faulty route. The PIA will restore it and be paid restoration charges according to pre-defined rates (Table-2 of Section-VII).

43. Network Upgradation & Site Survey

The core objective of this project is transforming the network to be "carrier-grade".

  • The Upgradation Mandate: The PIA must replace existing GPON equipment with IP-MPLS routers (Access/Aggregator) and upgrade the existing linear OFC network into a highly redundant Ring Topology.

  • GIS-Based Survey & Planning:

    • The PIA must deploy a GIS-based planning tool for desktop planning to find the optimal/shortest path before conducting the actual physical survey.

    • The PIA must record photographic/video evidence of any route constraints that force a deviation from the desktop plan.

    • The PIA must create and maintain As-Built Diagrams (ABD), L-14 diagrams, High-Level Designs (HLD), and Low-Level Designs (LLD) on a GIS platform accessible to BSNL.

44. Network Design & Ring Architecture

  • Ring Size: The PIA must design a redundant OFC network connecting all GPs in a ring architecture. A standard GP ring should normally consist of 8 to 10 GPs.

  • Child Rings: If a direct GP ring from the Block is not feasible due to distance or other constraints, the PIA can plan "Child Rings" (sub-rings) with the approval of BSNL and the Independent Engineer (IE).

  • Linear Exceptions: BSNL may allow linear topology instead of a ring for a maximum of up to 10% of GPs in a circle, strictly based on feasibility and IE recommendations.

45. Alternate Technology Implementation & Strict Caps

If neither Underground (UG) nor Aerial (ADSS) OFC is physically possible, BSNL may allow the use of Unlicensed Band Radio (UBR) or Digital Microwave (DMW). However, this is strictly capped:

  • Max 2% of GPs: UP (East & West), Bihar, Haryana, Punjab.

  • Max 4% of GPs: Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, West Bengal.

  • Max 6% of GPs: Kerala, Karnataka, Assam.

  • Max 15% of GPs: Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand.

  • Max 20% of GPs: Jammu & Kashmir, NER-I, NER-II.

  • Note: The PIA must supply, install, and commission these radio links. For cases exceeding these caps, BSNL will float a separate tender, but the PIA must facilitate its integration into the State NOC.

46. Maintenance (FLM) Rates for Alternate Technology & SLAs

The PIA is responsible for the First Line Maintenance (FLM) of these radio/microwave links throughout the 10-year contract. BSNL will pay the PIA fixed monthly rates per link:

  • Standard Regions: Rs. 1,000/month (Years 1-5) and Rs. 1,300/month (Years 6-10).

  • Hilly Regions (J&K, UK, HP, NE1, NE2): Rs. 1,500/month (Years 1-5) and Rs. 1,800/month (Years 6-10).

  • Strict Trouble Ticket SLA: The PIA must attend to trouble tickets within 8 hours. Penalties escalate heavily for delays beyond 8, 24, 48, and 72 hours (e.g., Rs. 100 to Rs. 400 per ticket/day).

47. Carrier-Grade Network Capabilities

The network designed by the PIA must be robust, fault-tolerant, and capable of handling future demands from Day 1. It must support:

  • Redundancy across nodes and optimal link utilization.

  • All types of data/voice services, including 2G, 3G, 4G, and 5G wireless backhaul.

  • FTTx, Layer-2/Layer-3 VPNs, IPTV, and Multicast VPNs.

  • End-to-end integration up to the State-Wide Area Network (SWAN) and National Knowledge Network (NKN).

This section covers the OFC Implementation Rules (including Leasing options), the strict Videography Mandates for Construction, the setup of the State Network Operations Centre (S-NOC), and Electronics Installation.

1. OFC Implementation & Leasing Options for Uncovered GPs

To connect approximately 47,000 currently uncovered GPs, the PIA has the option to lay new fiber or utilize/lease existing optical fiber cables from other TSP/ISP/IP-1 license holders.

  • Conditions for Leasing OFC:

    • The lease must cover the complete ring from the Block to the GPs.

    • The PIA remains fully responsible for end-to-end SLAs and Operations & Maintenance (O&M) for the entire 10-year contract period.

    • Ownership Transfer: At the end of the 10-year contract, the ownership of the complete leased ring (OFC + PLB HDPE duct) must be transferred to USOF at a nominal price of Rs. 1.00 per Km.

    • A tripartite agreement must be signed between the PIA, the fiber owner, and BSNL.

  • Payment Structure for Leased OFC (Capex + Opex for 10 years):

    • 48F UG OFC (with duct) on lease: Paid at 1.6 times the standard rate of X2 (from Price Schedule).

    • 24F ADSS Aerial OFC on lease: Paid at 1.6 times the standard rate of X3.

  • Right of Way (RoW) for Leased Aerial Fiber: If the leased 24F ADSS fiber does not utilize State Discom poles (e.g., the lessor erected their own poles), BSNL will pay ROW charges of Rs. 200 per pole per annum. BSNL will also facilitate new RoW permissions with State Governments for new construction.

2. Strict Videography & Photographic Evidence Mandates

To ensure construction quality and prevent fraud (like shallow trenching), BSNL mandates rigorous, geo-tagged videography and photography for all outside plant work. Payments will only be released if these files are submitted and verified.

  • Video Requirements (Open Trenches & HDD):

    • Trenches must be video recorded using a Vertical Measuring Stick (Staff) every 50 meters to clearly prove trench depth.

    • Entry and exit pits for Horizontal Directional Drilling (HDD) must be recorded.

  • Photographic Requirements: Geo-tagged, time-stamped photos are mandatory for OFC Blowing (showing drum/meter readings), Splicing (showing all 24/48 fibers in cassettes), Manholes/Chambers, and Route Markers.

  • Format & Size:

    • Videos must be in MP4 format featuring 3 concurrent windows:

      1. Physical work video with Lat/Long & Timestamp.

      2. Live location tracking on Google Maps.

      3. KMZ/KML file details (chainage, depth, offset).

    • Files must be under 30MB (for easy sharing) and cover at least 2 minutes of footage per 40 meters of trenching. Camera moving speed must not exceed 20 meters/minute.

    • Recordings must be done in clear daylight (no rain/fog).

  • File Nomenclature: Files must follow a strict naming convention: Route_Circle/Dist/Block_StartChainage_EndChainage: WorkType.

3. State Network Operations Centre (S-NOC) Setup

The PIA must establish a dedicated S-NOC in each state to manage, monitor, and troubleshoot both the existing and newly created networks 24x7x365.

  • S-NOC Infrastructure: The S-NOC must handle fault, performance, configuration, and security management. It must integrate with BSNL’s Business Exchange Gateway (BEG) within 90 days of the agreement.

  • Space & Rent:

    • The PIA must lease space in a BSNL exchange/building (~2500 Sq. ft. for Category 1 packages, ~2000 Sq. ft. for Category 2).

    • The rent will be decided by BSNL's Fair Rent Assessment Committee (FRAC) and paid by the PIA.

    • The PIA must install their own electrical infrastructure (UPS, backup generators) or tap into BSNL's power using a sub-meter. Electricity charges are reimbursed quarterly to the PIA.

  • Central NOC Integration: The S-NOC must be integrated with the central BharatNet NOCs in Delhi and Bangalore. BSNL will provide the Data Communication Network (DCN) links connecting Block routers to the S-NOC, and S-NOC to the Central NOC, free of charge to the PIA.

4. Electronics Installation & "Mini OLT" Hosting

  • IP-MPLS Routers: The PIA must supply, install, and test robust, carrier-grade electronics (routers) with adequate power backup to ensure network availability.

  • Integration: The PIA is responsible for integrating the existing BSNL Block OLTs with the newly installed Block routers.

  • Hosting BSNL’s "Mini OLTs":

    • BSNL is procuring "Mini OLTs" (1 RU size) under a separate tender to provide FTTH connections directly from the GPs.

    • The PIA is strictly required to provide space inside their GP equipment rack/cabinet to house these Mini OLTs.

    • The PIA must allow the Mini OLT to draw power from the PIA’s GP power system and facilitate its installation, testing, and First Line Maintenance (FLM).

    • The PIA must configure and integrate these Mini OLTs with the MPLS network to ensure last-mile connectivity.

This section details the critical mechanisms for Bandwidth Expansion, Last Mile Connectivity (FTTH) provisioning, Strict Measurement Rules, and the precise Payment Deductions for Shallow Trenching.

1. Network Augmentation & Dark Fibre Provisioning

  • Ring Capacity Expansion: The rings are initially designed for 10 Gbps covering 8-10 nodes. If bandwidth demands increase in the future, the PIA must upgrade capacity by splitting the rings using spare fibers and ports on the Block router. Any extra Block routers needed will be paid as per finalized rates.

  • Dark Fibre: The PIA must build and provision dark fiber end-to-end for commercial use or village connectivity.

  • Joint Operations for Dark Fibre: If the PIA has to open an existing OFC joint for the first time strictly to provide dark fiber (which includes fault localization, digging, splicing, and backfilling), BSNL will pay a one-time fee of Rs. 3,500 per joint. If BSNL orders the construction of a new joint chamber, an additional Rs. 4,000 will be paid.

2. Last Mile Connectivity (FTTH) Rules & Payments

BSNL intends to push fiber directly to villages from the nearest GP. The PIA is responsible for this Last Mile Connectivity.

  • Infrastructure: The PIA will lay a 6F Overhead OFC (using existing electricity poles wherever possible). If poles are unavailable, the PIA must erect wooden or GI poles.

  • Termination: The OFC must terminate at the GP's Fiber Termination Box (FTB) with a minimum 16-pair capacity to support BSNL's FTTH connections or Mini-OLTs.

  • Capex Payment: BSNL will pay the PIA a flat rate of Rs. 80,000 per Km (or Rs. 80 per meter) for deploying this last-mile overhead fiber.

    • 40% of this Capex is paid immediately after the successful commissioning of the first FTTH connection in that village.

  • O&M Payment for Last Mile: The remaining amount is paid as O&M charges spread over the next 8 years.

    • An annual payment of 7.5% of the Capex is divided into quarterly payments of 1.875%.

    • Strict SLA: This 1.875% is only paid if the quarter's uptime SLA is 95% or better. If uptime drops, the payment degrades rapidly (e.g., 85%-90% uptime = 1.4% payout). Below 65% uptime results in NIL payment for the quarter.

  • Provisioning Penalty: O&M payments for a Block will be frozen until all villages under Work Orders in that block are connected. A penalty of Rs. 1,000 per day applies for delays beyond 30 days.

3. Strict Measurement Rules & Measurement Book (MB)

BSNL and the Independent Engineer (IE) will maintain tight control over how trenching and cable lengths are measured for payment.

  • The Measurement Book (MB): Must be maintained in ink. No erasures are allowed. Mistakes must be crossed out, corrected, and initialed. The MB is compiled in real-time on a computer and checked weekly by the IE.

  • Trench Depth Measurement: Recorded every 10 meters in multiples of 5 cm (e.g., 97 cm is recorded as 95 cm; 103 cm as 105 cm). This is to discourage the tendency of workers to dig shallow trenches.

  • Cable Length Payment: The payment for cable laying/trenching is strictly based on the RODO Meter readings (the length of the pipe/duct laid), not the total length of the cable pulled (since extra cable is coiled/looped). OTDR readings are only used to identify cuts later.

  • Geo-Tagged Proof: The IE must take geo-tagged photographs of the depth measurement every 100 meters (for open trenches) and at the entry/exit pits for Horizontal Directional Drilling (HDD), uploaded directly to the BharatNet GIS App.

  • Double Penalty for False Data: If the sample data checked by the IE does not match the PIA's MB entry (up to a 5cm difference), BSNL will levy a penalty equal to the difference in payment, in addition to reducing the payment for the shallow depth.

4. Penalties and Payment Formulas for Shallow Trenching

Standard trench depth is 165 cm in plains and 120 cm in hilly areas. If the PIA encounters hard rock or utilities and cannot reach these depths, BSNL allows shallow trenching, but strictly reduces the payout using specific mathematical formulas and mandates extra protection (DWC, GI pipes, or RCC concrete).

Plain Area Deductions:

  • 165 to 150 cm: No extra protection needed. Payment reduced to: 150 * 95 / 165 %

  • 150 to 120 cm: No extra protection needed. Payment reduced to: 120 * 87.5 / 165 %

  • 120 to 100 cm: DWC pipe protection required. Payment reduced to: 100 * 75 / 165 %

  • 100 to 60 cm: GI pipe or Cement Concrete required. Payment reduced to: 60 * 60 / 165 %

  • 60 to 30 cm: RCC protection required. Payment reduced to: 30 * 60 / 165 %
    (Note: Digging below 100 cm in plains or 30 cm in hills requires special approval from the CGM).

Hilly Area Deductions (Base depth 120 cm):

  • 120 to 100 cm: No extra protection. Payment reduced to: 100 * 95 / 120 %

  • 100 to 80 cm: No extra protection. Payment reduced to: 80 * 87.5 / 120 %

  • 80 to 50 cm: DWC pipe required. Payment reduced to: 50 * 75 / 120 %

  • 50 to 30 cm: GI pipe or Cement Concrete required. Payment reduced to: 30 * 60 / 120 %

  • < 30 cm: RCC protection required. Payment reduced to: 20 * 60 / 120 %

5. Inspection and Acceptance Testing

  • During the project lifecycle (construction and O&M), BSNL and the IE retain the absolute right to inspect, test, and audit the network.

  • The IE will mandate Acceptance Testing (A/T) according to "Good Industry Practice" and the predefined Acceptance Testing Template.

  • This testing covers everything from passive infrastructure (trenches, cables) to the active electronics (routers) and the live traffic connecting the GP to the Block and the NOC.

This section details the critical transition from network construction to live operations, covering Acceptance Testing (PAT/FAT), Defect Rectification, Issuance of Certificates, and Comprehensive Operation & Maintenance (O&M) Rules.

1. Equipment Supply & Preliminary Testing

Before any equipment goes live, it must pass strict quality and performance gateways:

  • OEM Certification: The PIA must obtain relevant test results and certificates from the Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) proving the materials meet BSNL’s Technical Specifications. These must be submitted to the Independent Engineer (IE) and BSNL within 7 days of receipt.

  • Cost of Defective Material: If any supplied material fails inspection, the PIA bears 100% of the replacement costs.

  • Preliminary Acceptance Testing (PAT): The PIA must first self-conduct Preliminary Acceptance Testing on the created network infrastructure.

  • Offering for FAT: Once PAT is complete, and at least 10% of the Gram Panchayats (GPs) in a Block are pinging successfully to the Central NOC/S-NOC, the PIA can officially offer the block to the IE for the Final Acceptance Test (FAT).

2. Final Acceptance Testing (FAT) & Discrepancies

The Independent Engineer (IE) and BSNL conduct the FAT to ensure the network is carrier-grade.

  • Testing Process: The IE conducts visual, physical, and aggregated tests at the GP, Block, and NOC levels. The PIA must provide all testing instruments (like OTDRs) and bear the cost of any required excavation to check buried cables during these tests.

  • FAT Results: The IE/BSNL will grade the tested infrastructure as either "Satisfactory" or "Unsatisfactory".

  • The 10% Discrepancy Rule (Crucial):

    • If the IE finds more than 10% discrepancies across site routes or GP locations in a block (due to PIA negligence), the entire FAT may be rejected. The PIA must redo the work and resubmit.

    • If discrepancies are less than 10%, BSNL/IE may issue a Provisional Acceptance Certificate, attaching a "Punch List" of outstanding minor defects that need fixing.

3. Defect Rectification & Completion Certificates

  • Fixing the Punch List: The PIA is obligated to rectify any items on the Punch List in a time-bound manner to receive the final Completion Certificate.

  • Strict Timelines for Rectification:

    • If equipment is defective, the PIA must replace/fix it within 1 month (30 days) of the initial report, completely free of charge.

    • Upon receiving an official "Inspection Report" from the IE highlighting deficiencies, the PIA has only 7 days to rectify and remedy those specific defects.

  • Final Certification: Once all defects are closed and the network fully conforms to standards, the IE/BSNL will issue the final Completion Certificate.

4. Operation and Maintenance (O&M) Responsibilities

Once the network is commissioned, the PIA officially enters the O&M phase for the entire 10-year contract duration.

  • Scope of O&M: The PIA is responsible for maintaining:

    1. The existing leased BSNL OFC.

    2. The newly laid incremental OFC.

    3. First Line Maintenance (FLM) of existing GPON equipment (OLTs, ONTs).

    4. Solar Photo Voltaic (SPV) systems and batteries at the GPs.

    5. The newly deployed IP-MPLS Routers and S-NOC.

  • O&M Start Date: For newly connected GPs, O&M starts immediately upon commissioning. For the existing network, it starts from the date of handover.

  • 24x7x365 Support: The PIA must establish a governance framework and helpdesk matrix operating round-the-clock. They must also deploy dedicated fault resolution teams at the Block level.

  • Insurance against Disasters: The PIA is mandated to insure all GP equipment against theft, fire, flood, earthquake, and storms for the entire project duration.

5. SLA Monitoring & Capacity Augmentation

  • SLA Grace Period: To allow the PIA time to stabilize the network, Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and SLAs will be monitored and reported, but penalties will only be computed and applied after 6 months from the date of taking over the network.

  • Periodic Maintenance: The PIA must conduct scheduled periodic maintenance and corrective maintenance to ensure high network uptime.

  • Capacity Augmentation & End of Life (EoL):

    • If bandwidth demands require SFP (Small Form-factor Pluggable) upgrades, the PIA must replace/upgrade them on a payment basis.

    • If any supplied equipment reaches its End of Life (EoL) during the 10-year contract, the PIA is fully responsible for replacing it with equipment of equivalent or higher specifications.

This section is highly critical as it defines the strict Project Completion Milestones, Construction Liquidated Damages (LD), and the detailed Service Level Agreements (SLAs) & Penalties for the Operations & Maintenance (O&M) phase.

1. Project Completion Schedule & Milestones

The PIA is given exactly 3 years (1095 days) to complete the network upgradation and construction. Progress is strictly tracked through 5 milestones, calculated from the "Appointed Date" (Agreement signing). The target metric is the percentage of GPs commissioned in rings and visible in the Central NOC/EMS:

  • Milestone I (180th Day / ~6 Months): 10% of total GPs in the package.

  • Milestone II (360th Day / ~12 Months): 30% of total GPs in the package.

  • Milestone III (540th Day / ~18 Months): 50% of total GPs in the package.

  • Milestone IV (720th Day / ~24 Months): 75% of total GPs in the package.

  • Milestone V (900th Day / ~30 Months): 90% of total GPs in the package.

  • Scheduled Completion Date (1095th Day / 36 Months): The project is officially considered complete when at least 90% of the total GPs in the package are visible in the Central NOC.

2. Construction Phase Penalties (Liquidated Damages)

If the PIA misses the targets for any of the above milestones, BSNL will levy heavy Liquidated Damages (LD) based on the value of the incomplete GPs:

  • Weeks 1 to 10 of delay: Penalty is 0.5% of the value of incomplete GPs per week (or part thereof).

  • Weeks 11 to 20 of delay: Penalty increases to 0.7% of the value of incomplete GPs per week.

  • Maximum LD Cap: The total LD is capped at 12% of the package cost.

  • Severe Default: If the PIA misses a milestone and still hasn't caught up by the time the next milestone arrives, BSNL can forfeit their Performance Bank Guarantee (PBG), impose penalties, or short-close/terminate the contract.

3. O&M SLAs for the Existing Network

Before the new IP-MPLS rings are fully built, the PIA must maintain the existing legacy network handed over by BSNL. SLAs are calculated Block-wise on a monthly basis.

  • Uptime Targets:

    • Hilly Areas: Minimum 98% uptime.

    • Plain Areas: Minimum 99% uptime.

  • Exclusions from Downtime: BSNL will not penalize the PIA if the downtime is caused by:

    • Force Majeure (natural disasters, floods, etc., if reported within 7 days).

    • Grid power failure (once the GP battery backup is legitimately exhausted).

    • Approved maintenance windows (up to 8 hours per link per month, capped at 1% of links per month).

4. O&M Penalty & Incentive Structure

If the PIA fails to meet the uptime targets, penalties are deducted directly from their quarterly O&M payouts.

  • The 6-Month Grace Period: For the first 6 months after taking over the legacy network from BSNL, no penalties will be applied provided the PIA keeps the uptime above 90%. If it falls below 90% during this grace period, penalties will apply.

  • Uptime Penalty Slabs (Example for Plain Areas):

    = 99%: No Penalty

    • 97% to < 99%: Proportionate penalty (e.g., 97.6% uptime = 1.4% penalty)

    • 95% to < 97%: Flat 6% penalty

    • 90% to < 95%: Flat 12% penalty

    • ... dropping all the way to a 100% penalty (zero payout) if uptime is below 60%.

  • Prolonged Outage Penalty: If any single GP ONT is down for more than 30 days, an additional penalty of Rs. 1,000 per week will be levied on top of standard downtime penalties.

  • Unreported Damage Penalty: If the PIA fails to report route damage, a penalty of Rs. 50 per meter of damaged route will be charged.

5. OFC Cut Rule: Penalties vs. Incentives

BSNL actively tracks how many GPs go down due to fiber cuts in a Block per month.

  • Allowance: BSNL allows 1 cut event per month affecting up to 40% of GPs in a Block without extra penalty.

  • The Penalty: If OFC cuts take down more than 40% of GPs in a Block in a single month, a penalty of Rs. 200 per event is levied.

  • The Incentive: If the PIA manages the network exceptionally well, and more than 80% of GPs in a block experience zero fiber cuts in a month, BSNL will pay the PIA an incentive of Rs. 200 per GP for every GP above the 80% threshold.

6. S-NOC Availability SLAs

The State Network Operations Centre (S-NOC) is the brain of the network and is held to an extremely high standard:

  • Target Uptime: 99.9% (No penalty).

  • Slight Drop (99.5% to < 99.8%): Proportionate penalty.

  • Moderate Drop (99% to < 99.5%): Twice the proportionate penalty.

  • Major Drop (95% to < 99%): Thrice the proportionate penalty.

  • Severe Drop (< 95%): Five times the proportionate penalty.

This section is highly critical as it defines the strict Project Completion Milestones, Construction Liquidated Damages (LD), and the detailed Service Level Agreements (SLAs) & Penalties for the Operations & Maintenance (O&M) phase.

1. Project Completion Schedule & Milestones

The PIA is given exactly 3 years (1095 days) to complete the network upgradation and construction. Progress is strictly tracked through 5 milestones, calculated from the "Appointed Date" (Agreement signing). The target metric is the percentage of GPs commissioned in rings and visible in the Central NOC/EMS:

  • Milestone I (180th Day / ~6 Months): 10% of total GPs in the package.

  • Milestone II (360th Day / ~12 Months): 30% of total GPs in the package.

  • Milestone III (540th Day / ~18 Months): 50% of total GPs in the package.

  • Milestone IV (720th Day / ~24 Months): 75% of total GPs in the package.

  • Milestone V (900th Day / ~30 Months): 90% of total GPs in the package.

  • Scheduled Completion Date (1095th Day / 36 Months): The project is officially considered complete when at least 90% of the total GPs in the package are visible in the Central NOC.

2. Construction Phase Penalties (Liquidated Damages)

If the PIA misses the targets for any of the above milestones, BSNL will levy heavy Liquidated Damages (LD) based on the value of the incomplete GPs:

  • Weeks 1 to 10 of delay: Penalty is 0.5% of the value of incomplete GPs per week (or part thereof).

  • Weeks 11 to 20 of delay: Penalty increases to 0.7% of the value of incomplete GPs per week.

  • Maximum LD Cap: The total LD is capped at 12% of the package cost.

  • Severe Default: If the PIA misses a milestone and still hasn't caught up by the time the next milestone arrives, BSNL can forfeit their Performance Bank Guarantee (PBG), impose penalties, or short-close/terminate the contract.

3. O&M SLAs for the Existing Network

Before the new IP-MPLS rings are fully built, the PIA must maintain the existing legacy network handed over by BSNL. SLAs are calculated Block-wise on a monthly basis.

  • Uptime Targets:

    • Hilly Areas: Minimum 98% uptime.

    • Plain Areas: Minimum 99% uptime.

  • Exclusions from Downtime: BSNL will not penalize the PIA if the downtime is caused by:

    • Force Majeure (natural disasters, floods, etc., if reported within 7 days).

    • Grid power failure (once the GP battery backup is legitimately exhausted).

    • Approved maintenance windows (up to 8 hours per link per month, capped at 1% of links per month).

4. O&M Penalty & Incentive Structure

If the PIA fails to meet the uptime targets, penalties are deducted directly from their quarterly O&M payouts.

  • The 6-Month Grace Period: For the first 6 months after taking over the legacy network from BSNL, no penalties will be applied provided the PIA keeps the uptime above 90%. If it falls below 90% during this grace period, penalties will apply.

  • Uptime Penalty Slabs (Example for Plain Areas):

    = 99%: No Penalty

    • 97% to < 99%: Proportionate penalty (e.g., 97.6% uptime = 1.4% penalty)

    • 95% to < 97%: Flat 6% penalty

    • 90% to < 95%: Flat 12% penalty

    • ... dropping all the way to a 100% penalty (zero payout) if uptime is below 60%.

  • Prolonged Outage Penalty: If any single GP ONT is down for more than 30 days, an additional penalty of Rs. 1,000 per week will be levied on top of standard downtime penalties.

  • Unreported Damage Penalty: If the PIA fails to report route damage, a penalty of Rs. 50 per meter of damaged route will be charged.

5. OFC Cut Rule: Penalties vs. Incentives

BSNL actively tracks how many GPs go down due to fiber cuts in a Block per month.

  • Allowance: BSNL allows 1 cut event per month affecting up to 40% of GPs in a Block without extra penalty.

  • The Penalty: If OFC cuts take down more than 40% of GPs in a Block in a single month, a penalty of Rs. 200 per event is levied.

  • The Incentive: If the PIA manages the network exceptionally well, and more than 80% of GPs in a block experience zero fiber cuts in a month, BSNL will pay the PIA an incentive of Rs. 200 per GP for every GP above the 80% threshold.

6. S-NOC Availability SLAs

The State Network Operations Centre (S-NOC) is the brain of the network and is held to an extremely high standard:

  • Target Uptime: 99.9% (No penalty).

  • Slight Drop (99.5% to < 99.8%): Proportionate penalty.

  • Moderate Drop (99% to < 99.5%): Twice the proportionate penalty.

  • Major Drop (95% to < 99%): Thrice the proportionate penalty.

  • Severe Drop (< 95%): Five times the proportionate penalty.

This section is highly critical as it details the Network Availability SLAs for the new Ring Topology, Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) penalties, and the complete Payment Milestones & Mobilization Advance structure for the project.

1. Network Availability SLAs for New Ring Topology

Once the new IP-MPLS ring network is deployed, BSNL imposes strict uptime targets at both the Block and GP levels. Network availability is calculated excluding planned downtime.

  • Block Level SLA (Block Router):

    • Target: ≥ 99.5% uptime (No Penalty).

    • ≥ 97.5% to < 99.5%: Proportionate Penalty.

    • ≥ 95.0% to < 97.5%: Twice the Proportionate Penalty.

    • < 95.0%: 1% additional penalty for every 0.25% reduction in uptime.

  • GP Level SLA (GP Router):

    • Target: ≥ 98% uptime (No Penalty).

    • ≥ 95% to < 98%: Proportionate Penalty.

    • ≥ 90% to < 95%: Twice the Proportionate Penalty.

    • < 90%: 3% additional penalty for every 1% reduction in uptime.

2. SLA Exclusions & Power Management

BSNL provides specific exclusions where the PIA will not be penalized for downtime:

  • Power/Electricity Issues: If the daily electricity availability is less than 8 hours, it is presumed the battery won't fully charge. The PIA should identify these sites during the survey and provision 1 KW Solar Panels.

  • Transformer/Grid Failures: If a transformer burns out, causing multi-day outages, the PIA is expected to deploy a 1 KVA portable DG set to keep the site running. BSNL grants a 24-hour SLA exclusion specifically to allow the PIA time to mobilize this generator to the GP.

  • Force Majeure: Exclusions for floods, landslides, storms, etc., are only granted if applied for via the NMS within 7 days and verified by the Independent Engineer (IE) with photographic/video evidence.

3. Service Provisioning & MTTR Penalties

  • Service Provisioning (from S-NOC): The PIA must provision new services from the State NOC within 48 hours.

    • Penalty: Rs. 10,000 per day for delays beyond 48 hours.

  • Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) for Fiber Cuts:

    • Target: Up to 8 hours (No Penalty).

    • 8 to 24 hours: Rs. 500 per additional hour.

    • > 24 hours: Rs. 1,000 per additional hour.

  • OFC Cut Limits per Block: BSNL limits the acceptable number of fiber cuts per month based on the block's route length (e.g., 1 cut allowed for ≤50km, 2 cuts for >50 to ≤100km).

    • Penalty: Rs. 1,000 per cut/fault exceeding the allowed limit.

4. Mobilization Advance

To assist with initial cash flow for equipment acquisition and mobilization, BSNL offers an advance payment:

  • Amount: Up to 10% of the CAPEX value of the Bid Project Cost.

  • Interest: It is a non-interest-bearing advance.

  • Security: The PIA must submit an Additional Bank Guarantee equal to 110% of the advance amount, valid until 60 days post-construction completion.

  • Recovery/Adjustment: The advance is recovered in 8 equal quarterly installments starting from the 9th month after disbursement.

    • Crucial Warning: If project milestones are delayed, penal interest (at SBI MCLR rate) will be charged on the delayed adjustment period.

5. CAPEX Payment Milestones

Payments for the construction phase are staggered based on strict commissioning milestones (certified by the IE):

  • 70% of Block/GP equipment cost: Paid upon commissioning of the respective routers.

  • 75% of OFC construction cost: Paid upon commissioning of individual rings (validated by IP ping).

  • Remaining stages (10% + 5% + 5% + 5%): Paid progressively as the network scales up (e.g., 90% GPs in a block visible, 90% of blocks completed, and final project completion).

  • Retention for Warranty: 10% of the payment for all active electronics (Routers, Microwave, NOC equipment, Batteries, etc.) is withheld and will only be paid after the completion of the 1-year warranty period.

6. Payment Rules for Leased OFC

If the PIA chooses to lease existing OFC from a TSP/ISP to connect uncovered GPs, the payment structure is totally different from newly constructed OFC:

  • Initial Payment: 20% of the due amount is paid after all GPs in the leased ring are commissioned.

  • Deferred Payments: The remaining 80% is paid in quarterly installments of 1.5% over a maximum of 10 years (minimum commitment is 7 years).

  • Asset Transfer: After 10 years, the ownership of the leased 24F/48F OFC must be transferred to USOF for a nominal price of Rs. 1.00 per Km.

  • Maintenance: The PIA must transparently maintain all fibers (live and dark) in the leased cable and is subject to the same SLA penalties as newly laid fiber.

This section covers the S-NOC Payment Milestones, the complex O&M Payment Calculations, Quality Assurance (QA) rules, Right of Way (RoW) execution, and Project Monitoring.

1. State NOC (S-NOC) Payment Milestones

Payments for the State Network Operations Centre (S-NOC) are staggered based on physical and operational readiness:

  • 30%: Upon physical infra readiness, delivery of hardware/software/licenses.

  • 30%: Upon Installation & Commissioning (A/T completion) and integration of at least 10% of GPs.

  • 15%: Upon integration with the Central BharatNet NOCs (Delhi & Bengaluru).

  • 15%: Upon overall project completion.

  • 10%: Retained and paid only after the completion of the 1-year warranty period.

2. O&M Payment Principles & Notional CAPEX

Operations & Maintenance (O&M) is paid quarterly on a per-GP basis.

  • Existing OFC Notional CAPEX: Since the PIA takes over existing BSNL fiber, BSNL assigns a "Notional CAPEX" to calculate O&M payouts for it.

    • Rs. 250/meter: Haryana, Punjab, Rajasthan, UP (E/W), MP, Bihar.

    • Rs. 300/meter: WB, Assam, Karnataka, Kerala.

    • Rs. 400/meter: J&K, HP, Uttarakhand, NE1, NE2.

  • O&M Rate (Year-Wise Matrix): The annual O&M payout is a fixed percentage (R) of the total CAPEX (Actual + Notional):

    • Years 1 to 5: 5.5% of CAPEX.

    • Years 6 to 10: 6.5% of CAPEX.

  • Invoice Processing: To ensure cash flow, BSNL will pay 75% of the O&M invoice within 45 days of receipt. The remaining 25% will be paid within 90 days after detailed audits and verifications.

3. Fiber Damage & Replacement Rules

The PIA must maintain the existing and newly laid fiber, but there is a strict threshold for what is covered under standard AMC versus what warrants extra payment:

  • Damages up to 200 meters: The PIA must repair or replace fiber damages up to 200 meters in a single stretch at their own cost (it is assumed to be covered within the standard AMC charges).

  • Damages exceeding 200 meters: If a major cut/damage exceeds 200 meters and is unavoidable, the PIA must notify the IE/BSNL. BSNL will issue a separate firm work order for the replacement. The PIA will be paid for this major replacement at 70% of the approved project rates (covering 100% of material costs and 50% of service costs).

4. Quality Assurance (QA) & Testing

  • TSEC/TAC Mandatory: All materials must have valid Type Approvals (TSEC/TAC) before shipment.

  • QA Charges: BSNL’s QA Circle will inspect the equipment. The PIA must initially pay the QA charges (which amount to 1% of the material cost). BSNL will then reimburse these charges to the PIA upon submission of the invoice.

  • Unlisted Components: If any minor component is required to make the system work but was not listed in the Bill of Materials (BoM), the PIA must supply it at no extra cost.

5. Right of Way (RoW) Rules & Reimbursements

Acquiring RoW (permissions to dig/lay cables) from Highway, Railway, and Forest authorities is often the biggest bottleneck in telecom projects.

  • PIA's Responsibility: The PIA must apply for, liaise, and obtain all RoW permissions. BSNL will only provide "introduction letters" to facilitate the process.

  • Financial Flow for RoW:

    • If the RoW charge is up to Rs. 50,000, the PIA pays it out-of-pocket and claims reimbursement from BSNL.

    • If the charge exceeds Rs. 50,000, the PIA can ask BSNL to pay the authority directly.

  • Reinstatement Charges: Statutory charges paid to local authorities for road restoration will be fully reimbursed by BSNL.

  • Penalty Relaxations: If the project is delayed purely due to pending RoW permissions (and not the PIA's negligence), the BSNL BA Head has the power to relax the delay penalties (LD).

6. Project Monitoring & IE Audits

BSNL and the Independent Engineer (IE) will heavily monitor the project using digital tools.

  • GUI Dashboard: The PIA must develop a graphical user interface (GUI) to report data and project progress to BSNL and the IE.

  • Development Phase Inspections: The IE will audit the network during construction. If an "Inspection Report" flags defects, the PIA must rectify them.

  • O&M Phase Inspections: During the 10-year operations phase, the IE will conduct a formal Six-Monthly Inspection/Audit of the network and KPIs. If defects are found, the PIA has 30 days to rectify them before the IE re-inspects.

This section covers the Testing Procedures, the extensive Roles and Responsibilities of the Independent Engineer (IE), and the Exit Management Plan for the end of the contract.

1. Acceptance Testing & Defect Rectification

To ensure the network meets "Good Industry Practice," testing is strictly monitored.

  • IE Participation: The Independent Engineer (IE) is required to participate in or witness at least 10% of all Acceptance Tests (A/T) conducted by the PIA.

  • Remedying Defects: If any test establishes a defect or deficiency in the network, the PIA has exactly 30 days to carry out remedial measures and submit a compliance report to the IE. All costs for re-testing and fixing defects are borne solely by the PIA.

  • Notification of Delays: If the IE determines that the PIA's progress is too slow to meet the scheduled milestones, BSNL will issue a notice. The PIA then has 15 days to submit a concrete plan detailing how they will expedite the work to get back on schedule.

2. The Independent Engineer (IE): Appointment & Authority

The IE acts as the crucial third-party auditor and technical authority for the entire 10-year lifespan of the project.

  • Appointment: BSNL/USOF will appoint the IE within 60 days of issuing the Purchase Order. The appointment is typically for a 3-year term, which is repeatedly renewed or reassigned.

  • Interim Arrangement: If BSNL delays appointing an IE, or if the IE quits/defaults, BSNL retains full power to designate its own personnel to discharge the IE's functions temporarily (except for certain specific approvals).

  • Dispute Resolution: If the PIA disagrees with the IE's advice, instructions, or decisions, it triggers the formal Dispute Resolution mechanism (DRC -> DAB -> OEC) outlined earlier in the contract.

3. Phase-wise Roles and Functions of the IE

The IE's responsibilities are vast and cover every phase of the DBOM (Design, Build, Operate, Maintain) contract:

  • Pre-Planning Phase: Validates the handover of the existing BSNL network to the PIA and reviews the PIA's O&M manuals.

  • Planning & Design Phase: Scrutinizes the GIS survey reports, validates the network design (especially the ring topology), and approves the Bill of Quantity (BOQ) for fiber and equipment.

  • Implementation Phase:

    • Inspects materials for quality.

    • Audits the installation of active equipment.

    • Performs the Final Acceptance Tests (FAT) across GPs and the State NOC.

    • Issues the crucial Provisional and Final Completion Certificates.

  • O&M Phase:

    • Validates network performance against SLAs and QoS parameters.

    • Calculates and recommends penalties for SLA non-compliance, which dictates the quarterly O&M payouts.

    • Conducts the formal Six-Monthly Inspection and Audit of the network.

  • Handback Phase: At the end of the 10 years, the IE validates the inventory and assets being transferred back from the PIA to BSNL.

4. Exit Management Plan

Because telecom networks are critical public infrastructure, the PIA cannot simply walk away when the contract ends or is terminated. A seamless transition is legally mandated.

  • Annual Updates: The PIA must draft and submit an Exit Management Plan to BSNL, and it must be updated annually to remain relevant.

  • Initial Submission: The first draft of the Exit Management plan must be submitted within 90 days of the Purchase Order issuance.

  • Scope of the Plan: It must detail how the PIA will communicate the transition to staff and BSNL, and how it will provide contingent support to BSNL or the replacement bidder for a reasonable period after the transfer.

5. Network Handover Timelines at Contract End

At the end of the contract (or upon termination), the PIA is given a strict 3-month (12 weeks) window to peacefully hand over the entire network back to BSNL.

  • Handover Schedule (From Date of Notice 'T'):

    • 25% of GPs and Blocks: T + 4 weeks

    • 50% of GPs and Blocks: T + 6 weeks

    • 75% of GPs and Blocks: T + 9 weeks

    • 100% of GPs and Blocks: T + 3 months

  • Asset Inventory Tracking: During this handover, the PIA must provide a highly detailed asset summary per package, documenting the exact number of connected GPs, kilometers of OFC laid, and the number of Block and GP routers installed.

This section concludes the Exit Management Plan and begins Section IV-C: Special Technical Conditions, detailing the exact technical standards the equipment must meet.

1. Exit Management: Physical Asset Handover

When the 10-year contract ends (or is terminated early), the PIA must physically hand over all created and maintained assets back to BSNL in working order.

  • GP & Block End Equipment: The PIA must hand over all Routers, SFPs, UPS/SMPS, Racks, Batteries, Solar Panels, Mini OLTs, and FDMS.

    • Condition check: The physical status of the equipment and the battery's OCV (Open Circuit Voltage) along with its replacement date must be officially noted in the Handover-Takeover (HOTO) memo.

  • OFC Network: The PIA must hand over the completely updated As-Built Diagrams (ABD) and L-14 route diagrams, jointly signed by the PIA, the Independent Engineer (IE), and BSNL. Any temporarily restored sections must be clearly marked.

  • State-NOC (S-NOC): The complete active and passive infrastructure of the S-NOC must be transferred. This includes all OEM details, software licenses, Manufacturer Authorization Forms (MAFs), network diagrams, administrative passwords, and keys. If there is any custom application without support, the PIA must hand over the source code to BSNL.

2. Exit Management: Network Condition & OTDR Testing

BSNL ensures that the PIA does not let the network degrade towards the end of the contract.

  • The "Equal or Better" Rule: The optical fiber parameters at the time of handover back to BSNL must be at least as good as, or better than, the condition they were in when the PIA initially took them over (e.g., optical power strictly between -8 dBm to -25 dBm at the GP).

  • Final OTDR Measurement: At the end of the contract, OTDR (Optical Time-Domain Reflectometer) measurements will be taken on all spare fibers.

    • Penalty: If the number of fiber events (splices/cuts) has increased compared to Day 1, the cost of rectification will be deducted from the PIA's final bill or security deposit.

  • Data & Confidentiality: The PIA must promptly supply all performance data, customer data, and Intellectual Property documentation to BSNL. The PIA must then securely return or destroy any confidential BSNL data left in their possession.

  • Transfer of Contracts: BSNL can force the PIA to transfer/assign any third-party equipment leases, software licenses, or maintenance contracts directly to BSNL or the new incoming replacement bidder.

3. Special Technical Conditions - Overview

This page introduces Section IV-C, which outlines the mandatory technical specifications for the entire DBOM project. It acts as an index for the subsequent annexures.

  • Scope of Specifications: It covers the Network architecture, Routers, Element Management System (eMS), State-NOC, Remote Fibre Management System (RFMS), Racks, UPS, Unlicensed Band Radio (UBR), and Digital Microwave.

  • Annexures Linked:

    • Annexure A: TEC GR Standards for passive material.

    • Annexure B: Detailed specs for Active Network components.

    • Annexure C: Engineering Instructions (EI) for Underground OFC laying.

    • Annexure D: GIS Mapping specifications.

4. Annexure A: Technical Specifications & TEC-GR Standards

BSNL strictly mandates that all supplied materials conform to the Telecom Engineering Centre Generic Requirements (TEC-GR). Bidders cannot use substandard or non-certified materials.

  • Underground OFC: Must be Armoured Optical Fibre Cable (TEC 85170:2011). While standard G.652D fiber is required, BSNL allows the use of bend-insensitive G.657A1 fiber.

  • Aerial OFC (ADSS): 48F/24F All-Dielectric Self-Supporting cable (TEC 85190:2022).

    • Special Rule: For Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh packages, the cable must be specifically rated for ice loading.

  • Last Mile/Drop Cable: 24F Aerial Drop OFC (TEC 85200:2022) for connecting villages from the GP.

  • Power Equipment:

    • Solar Panels: Must meet TEC/GR 66080:2016.

    • Batteries: BSNL strictly specifies the use of Lithium-Ion Batteries (2400 VAH) meeting TEC 67030:2016. (This is a major shift from older VRLA batteries to ensure longer life and better performance in rural areas).

  • Passive Accessories: Fixed Attenuators, Optical Splitters (1:2 to 1:16), Patch Cords, Splice Closures, and Fibre Termination Boxes (FTBs) must all strictly adhere to their respective latest TEC-GR standards listed on this page.

Let me know when you are ready to proceed to the next 5 pages (Pages 130 to 134)!

title: Creation of SES in SAP
content:
1. Initiate Transaction
Transaction Code (Tx Code): ML81N

2. Select Purchase Order

  • Click Other PO

  • Enter PO No (Purchase Order Number)

  • Click on Enter/Check (✔ icon)

3. Create Entry Sheet

  • Click on Create Entry Sheet (Paper icon)

  • Short Text: Fill up the short text (e.g., "OFC BRK...")

4. Enter Header Details (Tab by Tab)

  • Inside Basic Data tab:

    • Service Location: [Enter Location]

    • Period: Enter [Fault Date] - to - [Restore Date]

  • Inside Accept Data tab:

    • Doc Text: Enter [Invoice No. with Date] / Work order number with date

  • Inside Long Text tab:

    • Long Text: Enter Long text contents.

  • Inside Addnl Data (Additional Data) tab:

    • Stor. Location: 7016

    • Reference Circle: 1035

5. Service Selection

  • Click Service Selection

  • Tick/Select Adopt full quantity

  • Click Adopt/Check (✔ icon with box)

6. Verification & Save

  • Check the Amount without tax

  • Click on Services

  • Click SAVE

7. Completion & Handover

  • Forward the SES to DE Sir (Final release will be done by him).

  • Pass Order Request: Write pass order request with Invoice No, Date, and Approved Amount in the text.

[ Start: Tx Code ML81N ]
↓
[ Click "Other PO" ] → [ Enter PO No ] → [ Click ✔ ]
↓
[ Click "Create Entry Sheet" (Paper Icon) ]
↓
[ Fill Short Text ] — ("OFC BRK...")
↓
[ Basic Data Tab ] — (Period: Fault Date - Restore Date)
↓
[ Accept Data Tab ] — (Doc Text: Invoice No with Date)

↓
[ Long Text Tab ] — (Enter the contents)
↓
[ Addnl Data Tab ] — (Stor. Loc: 7016 | Ref Circle: 1035)
↓
[ Click "Service Selection" ]
↓
[ Tick "Adopt Full Quantity" ] → [ Click ✔ ]
↓
[ Check Amount (without tax) ]
↓
[ Click Services ]
↓
[ SAVE ]
↓
[ Forward to DE Sir for Final Release ]
↓
[ Write Pass Order Request ] — (Invoice No, Date & Approved Amount)
